coredata.dc:title: Lithium-ion battery degradation modelling using universal differential equations: Development of a cost-effective parameterisation methodology

Damoulas, Theodoros
Planella, Ferran Brosa
Widanage, W. Dhammika
coredata.dc:description: The accuracy and reliability of physics-based lithium-ion battery degradation models are limited by incomplete understanding of degradation mechanisms. This article presents Universal Differential Equations (UDE) based degradation modelling, which integrates neural networks into physics-based model differential equations to learn partially understood degradation mechanisms. Therefore, this approach combines the function approximation capabilities of machine learning with the interpretability of physics-based models. However, the widespread adoption of this methodology is hindered by the high cost of training neural networks placed within a physics-based degradation model. To address this, we propose a cost-effective parameterisation method that exploits the large difference between electrochemical and degradation time scales, to speed up the gradient calculation using the continuous adjoint sensitivity analysis. Additionally, efficient scaling of this method to multiple ageing datasets is ensured through mini-batching. Finally, we demonstrate this approach by developing a novel UDE calendar ageing model and validating it against in-house experimental data covering 39 storage conditions (13 states of charge at 0 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C). The predictions on full cell capacity and loss of active material (LAM) at negative electrode align well with experimental observations with an average RMSE of 0.66% and 1.11% respectively, which was a significant improvement over the baseline physics-based model.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accuracy and reliability of physics-based lithium-ion battery degradation models are limited by incomplete understanding of degradation mechanisms. This article presents Universal Differential Equations (UDE) based degradation modelling, which integrates neural networks into physics-based model differential equations to learn partially understood degradation mechanisms. Therefore, this approach combines the function approximation capabilities of machine learning with the interpretability of physics-based models. However, the widespread adoption of this methodology is hindered by the high cost of training neural networks placed within a physics-based degradation model. To address this, we propose a cost-effective parameterisation method that exploits the large difference between electrochemical and degradation time scales, to speed up the gradient calculation using the continuous adjoint sensitivity analysis. Additionally, efficient scaling of this method to multiple ageing datasets is ensured through mini-batching. Finally, we demonstrate this approach by developing a novel UDE calendar ageing model and validating it against in-house experimental data covering 39 storage conditions (13 states of charge at 0 °C, 25 °C, and 45 °C). The predictions on full cell capacity and loss of active material (LAM) at negative electrode align well with experimental observations with an average RMSE of 0.66% and 1.11% respectively, which was a significant improvement over the baseline physics-based model.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The Julia code for the models used in this article is available on the GitHub repository https://github.com/JishnuKuzhiyil/Calendar-Ageing-Modelling-using--UDE---Julia-Code.git , and the experimental calendar ageing dataset is provided in the Zenodo repository https://zenodo.org/records/14577286 .

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are important in humanity’s progress towards a sustainable, low-carbon future. Mathematical models of LIBs help engineers to create optimal designs for battery-powered systems, and also to make informed decisions about their safe and efficient operation. For example, during the design phase of an electric aircraft, model-based optimisation is used to make informed choices on the design of its battery pack, thermal management system, charging protocols etc.

Among various LIB modelling approaches, continuum physics-based models stand out, due to the high degree of physical insight they offer [1]. Continuum models consist of differential algebraic equations that describe the conservation and transport laws of charge and mass species within the battery [2]. Once formulated and parameterised, these models can be integrated with platforms like PyBaMM [3] to run virtual experiments that simulate real-world battery operations and predict corresponding battery responses.

Continuum battery modelling starts with an electrochemical model, such as the Doyle–Fuller–Newman (DFN) model, which describes the transport of lithium within the battery [4]. To simulate battery degradation, additional sub-models that represent relevant degradation mechanisms must be coupled with the electrochemical model [5,6]. The accuracy of long-term simulations relies on how accurately these degradation mechanisms are represented. However, battery degradation mechanisms are not yet fully understood and remain an active area of research [7,8]. This incomplete understanding of the degradation physics could compromise the accuracy of the physics-based degradation models and of any subsequent decisions made about the safe and efficient operation of batteries [9].

The traditional approach to addressing this challenge involves conducting more scientific studies to understand the missing physics and subsequently incorporating them into the continuum models. However, this method is prohibitively time-consuming and expensive. Moreover, due to the complex nature of battery degradation mechanisms, even extensive research may not guarantee a complete and correct model of all the relevant physics. The emerging field of scientific machine learning (SciML) offers an alternative solution [10,11].

SciML is a modelling philosophy that combines mechanistic information from a physics-based model (i.e., causation) with correlations from data to account for unknown physics [12,13]. In particular, the SciML framework of Universal Differential Equations (UDEs) allows the integration of neural networks within the state equations of a physics-based model [13,14]. The UDE approach can be used to improve continuum degradation modelling by using neural networks to enhance sub-models describing poorly understood degradation mechanisms. Within this setting, the learning of missing physics becomes equivalent to the parameterisation of the UDE model.

Although articles [15] and [16] have used UDE for battery degradation modelling, they have not fully addressed the challenge of improving models that describe degradation mechanisms using measurable data. For example, [15] did not use neural networks to improve any degradation mechanism, but instead used them to model the residual of aggregate quantities such as capacity and resistance, which the physics-based model failed to capture. Although this formulation improved the accuracy of capacity and resistance predictions, it did not improve the accuracy of other important outputs, such as degradation modes. On the other hand, [16] demonstrated the ability of the UDE framework to model the growth of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) during cell formation. However, they considered synthetic data of internal battery variables generated using a physics-based model, which are not measurable in practice.

This article aims to show how the UDE framework can be used to improve submodels that describe degradation mechanisms within a continuum electrochemical degradation model using real, measurable data. We identified the high cost of parameterising the UDE degradation model as a major roadblock to achieving this goal. The parameterisation is performed as an optimisation problem, which requires numerous forward runs of the UDE degradation model. However, each forward run is expensive due to the coupling between the electrochemical and degradation models [4]. The coupling constrains the numerical solver to take time steps on the time scale of electrochemical dynamics (spanning seconds to minutes), while the ageing dynamics span over months. Thus, coupling makes the overall optimisation process highly expensive (see Section 3.1 for a detailed explanation). To advance UDE degradation research, it is imperative to develop a faster and more efficient parameterisation methodology.

To this extent, this research article develops a novel approach suitable for parameterising UDE degradation models. The methodology comprises two key components. First, inspired by the work of Sulzer et al. [17], we created a methodology that facilitates faster gradient calculation by exploiting wide differences in electrochemical and degradation time scales. This method is suitable when a single ageing dataset is considered. The second component involves mini-batching techniques [18], which enable efficient scaling of the optimisation when multiple ageing datasets are present. The combination of these two strategies allows faster and cheaper parameterisation of UDE degradation models.

This article demonstrates the formulation and parameterisation of a UDE degradation model that explains calendar ageing. Calendar ageing refers to battery degradation under zero load conditions [19,20]. Battery-powered devices, including electric vehicles and most consumer electronics, spend
≥ 90 %
of their lifetime in an idle state, which emphasises the importance of developing accurate calendar ageing models [21]. The research used an in-house collected calendar ageing data set, hereafter referred to as the WMG-calendar ageing data set. The ageing experiment involved storing LGM-50T commercial cells, at 0, 25 and 45
° C
, and 13 different State of Charge (SOC) points per temperature.

The literature reports that continual growth of the Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) layer is the dominant mechanism causing calendar ageing [22,23]. We show that commonly used models for SEI growth, particularly the Tafel kinetics limited by the solvent diffusion [4,24], fail to accurately explain the trends observed in our experimental data. To rectify this, we introduce a UDE degradation model and parameterised it with our newly developed parameterisation method. The major contributions of the article are described below.

ce:list-item.ce:para: This article addresses the high computational cost of parameterising the UDE degradation model. The developed methodology exploits the wide difference in model time scales to accelerate the gradient calculation step involved during parameter optimisation. Furthermore, the mini-batching technique is used to efficiently scale the method when multiple ageing datasets are involved.

The limitations of the physics-based SEI growth model are shown by validating it against an extensive calendar ageing dataset, comprising of three storage temperatures and 13 storage SOCs per temperature. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to evaluate SEI models across such a wide range of ageing conditions.

This is the first UDE-based model for calendar ageing with ML models integrated within the state equations of a continuum physics-based degradation model. The developed UDE model outperformed the widely used physics-based models in accuracy across most storage conditions, including those not used for UDE training. On average, the RMSE of capacity and anode LAM predictions by the UDE model was 58.72% and 33.0% lower, respectively, compared to the physics-based model.

ce:section.ce:para: Section 2 systematically introduces the UDE degradation modelling framework. Section 3 explains the challenges associated with the parameterisation of the UDE degradation models. Additionally, Section 3 also explains the development of Accelerated Parameterisation via Timescale Decoupling (APTD) and mini-batching approaches to address the parameterisation challenges. Section 4 presents the calendar ageing dataset and develops physics-based and UDE degradation models for battery calendar ageing. Section 5 describes the parameterisation and validation of both models. Section 6 compares the presented calendar ageing modelling approach against traditional approaches. Finally, the conclusions of the study are presented in Section 7.

Physics-based degradation models are a combination of an electrochemical model coupled with additional differential equations describing the degradation mechanisms. To simulate long-term battery behaviour, both models must be solved simultaneously [1]. Let

The formulation of the function
h
( ⋅ )
is a crucial step in UDE degradation modelling. The modeller must make decisions such as how to retain the known physics, where to embed neural networks, and how to select their architecture, inputs, and outputs based on domain knowledge. As UDE degradation modelling research is in its early phase, standardised guidelines for designing the UDE degradation state function
h
( ⋅ )
do not exist. Progress in this field requires researchers to address individual degradation modelling problems and to progressively establish standardised guidelines and practices. As a step in this direction, this article performs calendar ageing modelling through the UDE framework. The process of constructing the UDE degradation state function for calendar ageing is explained in Section 4.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameterisation of the UDE degradation model

ce:section.ce:para:
Parameterisation of the UDE degradation model involves the estimation of θ in (2b), by optimising a loss function L that penalises the error between the predictions of the model and the experimental observations. Note that, it is during parameterisation, the neural networks learn the missing physics. Since the vector θ includes parameters of the neural networks, it is preferable to use gradient-based optimisers [25]. These optimisers require the calculation of the gradient
d L
d θ
, which in turn depends on the gradient of the solution of the UDE degradation model with respect to its parameters [13,26].

Continuous Adjoint Sensitivity (CAS) is the recommended method for calculating the gradient of differential equation solutions to its parameters, especially when a large number of parameters are present [27]. The gradient calculation using CAS involves solving the original differential equations in the forward time direction, followed by solving adjoint differential equations in the reverse time direction [13,26]. The solution of the adjoint differential equations enables the calculation of the required gradient, refer to Appendix A for a summary of adjoint calculations.

The parameterisation of UDE degradation models is challenging for two reasons. First, as a large number of parameters are involved, many iterations are needed during the optimisation. Second, the gradient calculation in each iterations is very time consuming. This is because CAS requires forward and inverse solving of a system of differential equations with
n
1
+
n
2
states for an extended simulation period (typically spanning months), while the adaptive solver is limited to taking time steps in the electrochemical time-scale range (seconds to minutes). The complexity further increases when the loss function involves multiple ageing datasets, as each iteration requires solving these equations for multiple conditions.

To rectify the challenges mentioned above, a novel parameterisation method is developed, consisting of two methods: Accelerated Parameterisation via Timescale Decoupling (APTD) and mini-batching. APTD allows for faster gradient calculation when a single ageing dataset is involved. Further, mini-batching enables efficient scaling of APTD when multiple ageing datasets are involved. Details of these methods are provided in Sections 3.2 and 3.3.

In this Section, we discuss the development of the APTD methodology, which enables faster parameterisation of UDE degradation models by accelerating the gradient calculation step. The APTD methodology is strongly inspired by Sulzer et al.’s adaptive inter-cycle acceleration algorithm [17]. Their algorithm exploits the substantial time-scale difference between electrochemical and degradation dynamics to speed up physics-based degradation model simulations, by about two orders of magnitude. Building on [17], Pannala et al. developed a faster parameterisation methodology for physics-based degradation models using a derivative-free optimiser [28]. However, derivative-free optimisers are not suitable for UDE degradation models, due to their large number of parameters.

The APTD methodology presented here extends Pannala’s methodology to make it suitable for UDE degradation modelling. As mentioned in Section 3.1, slowness of gradient calculation arises from the solver’s limitation in taking smaller time steps for extended simulations, a consequence of the coupling between electrochemical and degradation dynamics. APTD reformulates the coupled system in Eq. (2b) into a new system of equations containing only the degradation states, allowing the numerical solver to take larger time steps, enabling faster gradient calculation.
The APTD methodology comprises the following three steps.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Estimation of the degradation states
z
( t )
from the ageing dataset.
Reformulation of the degradation differential equation (Eq. (2b)) for faster gradient calculation.

ce:section.ce:para:
The first step involves estimating
z
( t )
using an ageing dataset and the known electrochemical model in Eq. (2a). Note that, degradation state variables often show linear, self-limiting or accelerating trends over time, which can be approximated using lower-order polynomials [5,17]. Keeping this in mind, two approaches can be introduced.

Approach 1: Estimate z at discrete time points by performing multiple independent parameter estimations on Eq. (2a), using dataset over short time intervals where z remains fairly constant. Then, approximate the continuous
z
( t )
with a lower-order polynomial. This approach is suitable for state variables, like the active material volume fraction, that can be estimated through degradation mode quantification [29,30].

Approach 2: Assume a parametric function for
z
( t )
with very few parameters based on prior knowledge and substitute it in Eq. (2a). Then estimate its parameters using ageing data. For example, SEI layer thickness shows a self-limiting trend [4] and can be approximated using a logistic function or square root function. This approach is more suitable for degradation state variables which cannot be directly estimated from degradation mode quantification, such as SEI layer thickness.

In practice, the modeller can employ both approaches, choosing the appropriate one for each element of z . Furthermore, modellers may also explore other approaches but the main goal remains estimating
z
( t )

ˆ
( t )

Eq. (3) can be interpreted as the rate of change of z in the degradation time scale τ equals the average rate of change over a short time interval around τ . Since

is pre-calculated and stored in the augmented dataset, it acts as a known external input when solving Eq. (3). This means Eq. (3) can be solved independently of the electrochemical model, allowing the solver to take large time steps, making gradient calculation using CAS faster.

Further, the loss function can be formulated as,
(4)

). Thus, CAS method can be directly used to find the gradient (refer to Appendix A or [26]).

On the other hand, formulating the loss function based on capacity measurement would make the gradient calculation more challenging. This is because, in model based simulations, capacity is typically calculated as the time interval between two discrete events: when the battery voltage reaches its upper and lower cut-off values. Calculating the derivative of time difference between discrete events is not straightforward. This complexity explains why Pannala et al. [28] used derivative-free optimisers in their work, where they used a loss function based on capacity measurement.

Decoupling electrochemical and degradation dynamics during parameterisation, as implemented in APTD, offers another key advantage. It prevents the electrochemical model from crashing during optimisation, when the optimiser suggests parameter values that result in physically infeasible degradation states [31]. This makes the optimisation smoother and more robust.

In summary, the APTD method reformulates the gradient calculation to enhance its speed, ensures differentiability of the loss function, and makes the optimisation process smoother. 1
1 APTD is applicable to both cyclic and calendar ageing model parameterisation. During calendar ageing, as electrochemical dynamics is inactive, ODE solvers can use larger time steps even without APTD problem reformulation. However, APTD problem reformulation is still useful in calendar ageing as it create a differentiable loss function.

Mini-batching is a widely used machine learning model parameterisation approach, that has lately been applied for parameterising system of differential equations [18]. This section aims to introduce the effectiveness of the mini-batching to physics-based degradation modelling community, which typically relies on full-batch loss functions even when multiple ageing datasets are involved [28]. Although mini-batching is proposed here for parameterising the UDE degradation model, this approach is equally applicable to the parameterisation of physics-based models as well.

Mini-batching involves dividing the entire dataset into smaller mini-batches [14]. During each optimiser iteration, the gradient is calculated using the part of the full batch loss function associated with the data points in that mini-batch. During each iteration, an individual mini-batch is utilised, progressively passing through all the mini-batches in a single full cycle, referred to as an epoch. Multiple epochs are run until the specified termination criterion. This study treats each ageing dataset as a mini-batch, resulting in a total of
N
D
mini-batches. Since mini-batching only uses one ageing dataset per optimisation iteration, the gradient calculation cost does not increase with the number of datasets considered.

Mini-batching also offers several other advantages over full-batch optimisation. It introduces stochasticity in the gradient calculation, helping the optimiser to avoid sharp local minima and saddle points [32]. Additionally, as reported by [18], the overall parameterisation time for differential equation systems was reduced by order of magnitude when employing mini-batching instead of full-batch optimisation. Moreover, the frequent parameter updates in mini-batching facilitate a more efficient exploration of the parameter space compared to full-batch methods.

To summarise, the computational complexity of parameterising the UDE degradation model in Eq. (2b) with

This article demonstrates a calendar ageing model created using the UDE degradation modelling approach. Calendar ageing refers to the process of battery degradation during storage. Section 4.1 describes the in-house WMG calendar ageing dataset used for the modelling. Further, as discussed in Section 4.2, Differential Voltage Analysis (DVA) was performed to identify the dominant degradation mechanisms causing the calendar ageing. Based on insights from the DVA, an appropriate physics-based model was chosen from the literature, as discussed in Section 4.3. Finally, to address the inadequacies of the physics-based model (discussed in results Section 5.1), a UDE degradation model was developed, as explained in Section 4.4.

The in-house collected WMG calendar ageing dataset consists of 117 LGM-50T commercial cells. Cells were stored at three ambient temperatures ( 0 ,
25
, and 45 °C) and 13 storage SOCs per temperature, with three cells stored under each condition. The storage duration ranges between 496–770 days. Table 1 shows the number of days calendar-aged, the storage SOCs, and storage temperature under each condition. The variation in storage duration across different storage experiments was due to practical difficulties associated with long-term ageing experiments, including operational and resource constraints. The LGM-50T cells have an NMC-811 cathode and silicon-graphite composite anode with 2% volume fraction of silicon [33]. See [34,35] for a detailed description of cell properties.

During storage, the cells were kept at open circuit condition inside three different thermal chambers. To check the ageing status, reference performance tests (RPTs) were carried out during different stages of ageing. During the RPTs, the cells were transferred to different thermal chamber maintained at
25
°
C, and connected to Digatron cyclers. Detailed explanation of experimental setup and experimental procedure is provided in supplementary material Section S2. The RPTs included capacity measurement at a
C / 3
discharge rate and resistance measurement at different SOCs using pulse tests. After each RPT, the SOC definition was updated based on the latest
C / 3
capacity, and cells were brought to the storage SOC according to the updated definition. Some RPTs, named as the RPT extended (RPTx), also included a
C / 20
(dis)charge step, which allows for the quantification of degradation modes.

The capacity fade during calendar ageing is typically attributed to the continuous growth of the SEI layer [23]. The SEI growth is caused by parasitic side reactions that involve the reduction of the electrolyte at the anode. These side reactions lead to a stoichiometric drift between electrodes, resulting in a decrease in cell capacity [29]. Although the SEI side reactions should only cause loss of lithium inventory (LLI), recent studies have also reported loss of active material (LAM) in the electrodes [23,36,37]. Understanding the dominant degradation modes (DMs) is crucial for choosing appropriate degradation differential equations [7]. This study used differential voltage analysis (DVA) to gain insight into degradation modes [38].

Note that, calendar ageing rates are generally expected to increase monotonically with storage SOC [7]. However, our dataset reveals a non-monotonic trend at higher SOCs across all three temperatures (see Figs. 4–6). For instance, in the
45
° C
experiments, cells stored at 70% SOC showed faster degradation than those stored at 100% SOC (Fig. 4). This interesting trend has also been observed in other studies [21,36]. It is attributed to a reversible self-discharge mechanism, caused by oxalates shuttling between the anode and cathode, which diverts lithium away from SEI formation. See [21] for a detailed explanation.

DVA is a non-invasive characterisation technique that uses near-equilibrium voltage (
V
e
) and capacity ( Q ) data from a slow constant current (dis)charge to generate a differential voltage (DV) plot [39,40]. The DV plot shows
d
V
e
/ d Q
plotted against Q , and the peaks in the plot represent the phase equilibria of the individual electrodes. Fig. 1 presents the DV plot derived from a
C / 20
discharge data of a fresh LGM-50T cell, with the peaks attributed to the negative and positive electrodes. The process of assigning peaks to individual electrodes is explained in the supplementary material Section S3. In a DV plot, the horizontal distance between peaks corresponding to the same electrode is proportional to the capacity of that electrode [21,41]. Furthermore, the distance between peaks of negative and positive electrodes indicates electrode balancing. Consequently,
Q
n
and
Q
p
in Fig. 1 represent the capacities of the negative electrode and positive electrode respectively. Therefore, the LAM of each electrode can be quantified as,

Figs. 2(a)–2(c) show the ratio of

The aged state in these figures corresponds to the final RPTx, conducted on various days between the 483rd and 511th days, for cells stored under different conditions. Each point in Figs. 2(a)–2(c) is based on DVA performed on slow voltage data from 3 different cells stored under the same condition. The small error bars indicate that these features are minimally affected by measurement noise or errors in peak identification.

For cells stored at 0 °C (Fig. 2(a)) and 25 °C (Fig. 2(b)), the ratio of
Q
p
stays close to 100%. The slight fluctuations can be attributed to errors during peak identification. The
Q
p
ratio also remains close to 100% for cells stored at 45 °C, with a slight decrease of up to 3% observed for cells stored at 50% and 60% SOCs (Fig. 2(c)). Since, these two are the only cases where positive electrode LAM is observed and the changes are very small, the effect of positive electrode LAM is omitted in this study.

In contrast, the negative electrode capacity (
Q
n
) shows a significant decrease under all storage conditions considered, with the ratio closely matching the ratio of the total cell capacity (
Q
FC
). This suggests negative electrode LAM is dominant and cannot be disregarded. Zilberman et al. also reported significant negative electrode LAM and minimal positive electrode LAM during calendar ageing of a commercial cell with the same chemistry [36]. The negative electrode LAM can be explained by the SEI side reactions on silicon anode, which generate products that cause pore blockage [42,43]. The pore blockage hinders the access of the electrolyte to the negative electrode particles, resulting in the loss of the negative electrode active material.

SEI side reactions and pore blockage were identified as dominant degradation mechanisms. However, based only on DVA, it is not possible to distinguish between the individual contributions of these two mechanisms to the total drop in cell capacity. Therefore, both of these mechanisms are considered during calendar ageing modelling.

Physically-based degradation models consist of an electrochemical model coupled with differential equations that describe the relevant degradation mechanisms. This study uses the single particle model with electrolyte (SPMe) proposed in [4] as the electrochemical model. SPMe assumes that all particles within an electrode behaves identically, allowing them to be represented using a single particle. This approximation is valid for the moderate current rates considered in this study, which do not exceed
C / 3
. The SPMe model equations are briefly summarised in Section S1 of the supplementary material for completeness. The reader is referred to [4] for a more detailed understanding of the model. The SPMe model parameters for the LGM50 cells were taken from [34] and are summarised in Table S1.

As discussed in Section 4.2, SEI side reactions and associated pore blockage are identified as the dominant degradation mechanisms. The SEI growth is modelled using the Tafel kinetics limited by the solvent diffusion model proposed in [4]. The model describes the current density of the SEI side reactions (
J
SEI
) at the anode particle surface using Tafel kinetics. Additionally, the reaction rate is also controlled by the solvent diffusion through the SEI layer. Note that, the chosen model can represent two other widely used SEI growth models, the solvent kinetic-limited SEI model [45] and the solvent diffusion-limited SEI model [6,46], through specific parameter choices [47]. The model equations are presented in Eq. (7).

However, the solvent consumption model predicts equal LAM in both the anode and cathode, which contradicts our observation that cathode LAM is negligible. The results indicate that the physics-based model correctly explains the square root of time behaviour (self-limiting trend) of the capacity drop qualitatively. However, the values do not match the experimental data accurately for all the storage conditions considered. To address this drawback, we develop a UDE degradation model, as discussed in Section 4.4.

As discussed in Section 2, the electrochemical model remains the same as in the physics-based model, which is the Single Particle Model with Electrolyte (SPMe). The modifications were only made to the degradation equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameterisation and validation of physics-based model
ce:section.ce:para: This Section focuses on the implementation, parameterisation and validation of the physics-based model discussed in Section 4.3. The implementation starts with the spatial discretisation of the SPMe PDEs using the finite volume method (FVM) to derive a system of ODEs. The mesh consists of 10 elements per anode and cathode particle domains, 10 elements in the anode and cathode electrolyte domains, and 6 elements in the separator domain. The resulting ODE system is solved using the Rosenbrock23 solver from the DifferentialEquations.jl library in the Julia programming language [49]. The simulation involved the same conditions used for the ageing experiments, including the RPTs. To simulate the voltage hold phase in the RPTs, the ODE system is solved with constant voltage as an additional algebraic condition.

The next step involves estimating the tunable model parameters:
k
SEI
,
D
SEI
,
α
SEI
, and β , using capacity data from RPTs and LAM data from RPTxs. The loss function
L
phy
gives more weight to capacity data over LAM due to the higher frequency of RPTs compared to RPTxs. The loss function is:

are weights, which were chosen to be 0.75 and 0.25, respectively.

Data from cells stored at 30%, 50%, and 80% (
M = 3
) are used for the parameter estimation. To account for the temperature dependency, these parameters are independently estimated using data at 0 ,
25
, and
45
° C
. The particle swarm optimiser (PSO) from Metaheuristics.jl was used for performing the optimisation. The PSO parameters used were: population size of 15, cognitive coefficient of 2.0, social coefficient of 2.0, inertia weight of 0.8, and 100 iterations. The optimal parameters are presented in Table 3.

After estimating the model parameters, the physics-based model predictions are validated against the experimental data. Figs. 4–6 shows the comparison between capacity predictions of physics-based model (shown in red) and experimental data (shown in black) for cells stored at temperatures 45 °C, 25 °C, and 0 °C, respectively. In addition, the RMSE of capacity predictions by the physics-based model are shown (in red) in Fig. 7. Similarly, Fig. S7-S9 illustrate the comparison between the loss of active material (LAM) predictions of the physics-based model and the LAM predictions obtained by performing differential voltage analysis (DVA) on the experimental data from cells stored at temperatures 45 °C, 25 °C, and 0 °C, respectively. The corresponding RMSE of the anode LAM predictions are presented in Fig. 8.

As shown in Figs. 4–6, the experimental observed capacity trajectory shows a self limiting trend over time, which is a characteristic of SEI growth [4]. The physics-based model predicts this self limiting trend qualitatively, thanks to the solvent diffusion accounted in the SEI growth equations. However, the physics-based model fails to describe the capacity trend quantitatively across all conditions. At all three storage temperatures, the model predictions significantly differ from the experimental data, especially for storage SOCs below 30% and above 80%. Similarly, considering the anode LAM predictions (Fig. S7-S9), the physics-based model offer predictions aligning with experimental data for most SOCs, except at very low SOCs. At storage SOCs below 30%, the model significantly underestimates the anode LAM.

The poor generalisation of quantitative predictions indicate missing physics in the model. Specifically, according to Tafel kinetics, the rate of SEI side reactions has an inverse exponential relationship with SEI potential, which becomes equal to anode equilibrium potential during cell storage. The anode equilibrium potential has a sharp increase at low anode lithiation stoichiometries, particularly for full cell SOC below 30% [34]. Therefore, as per Tafel kinetics, the ageing rate should exhibit substantial reduction when they are stored at SOCs lower than 30% compared to being stored at 30% SOC. However, such a sharp decrease in ageing rate is not observed in the experimental data at all three temperatures considered (see Figs. 4–6).

This results in severe under-prediction of capacity loss and LAM at lower storage SOCs, resulting in high RMSE values (see Figs. 7 and 8). Furthermore, the exponential relation also causes the physics-based model to over predict the ageing rate at higher SOCs, especially noticeable for cells stored at 0 °C and 25 °C. It is worth noting that, other continuum SEI models such as the electron tunnelling and interstitial diffusion models also use this exponential dependence [47] and would suffer from similar issues.

To address the issue of the exponential relationship, we attempted to reduce the value of
α
SEI
inside the exponent. Lowering the
α
SEI
value makes the exponential curve more shallow. Although this approach improved accuracy at higher storage SOCs, even decreasing the
α
SEI
to 0.1 could not adequately explain the capacity drop trend observed at lower SOCs. Therefore, in this study, we maintained the values of
α
SEI
obtained from model parameterisation, which were around 0.5. Note that, in majority, if not all, of the previous studies,
α
SEI
≈ 0 . 5
was used. This supports our decision to retain this value.

As detailed above, model validation reveals the inadequacy of the physics-based model to explain the experimental trends adequately. This conclusion is unsurprising as the SEI growth is a highly complex phenomenon involving multiple reactions, whereas the Tafel kinetics model is a simplified representation that considers only a single representative reduction reaction.

This Section focuses on the parameterisation of the UDE degradation model proposed in Section 4.4. The parameterisation involves the estimation of the parameters of the neural networks
N
1
and
N
2
, along with the temperature-dependent rate constants
κ
1
and
κ
2
. To achieve this, we utilise the APTD methodology and the mini-batching approach described in Sections 3.2 and 3.3 respectively. Furthermore, appropriate feature engineering transformations (
Ψ
i
in Eqs. (12) and (13)) are also determined in this Section.

Note that, the neural networks
N
1
and
N
2
do not consider temperature as an input variable. Instead, the influence of temperature on
J
SEI
(Eq. (12)) and derivative of
ϵ
n
a
(Eq. (13)) is captured through the temperature-dependent rate constants
κ
1
and
κ
2
, respectively. This formulation allows neural network parameters to be estimated using data from cells stored at a single temperature, which in this study is 45 °C. For other temperatures, such as
0
° C
and
25
° C
, the same neural network parameters can be retained, and only the rate constants need to be updated. Without losing generality, we can set
κ
1
and
κ

equal to one at
45
° C
. For other temperatures, they can be estimated using data from just one or two storage conditions at that temperature.

To estimate neural network parameters, data from eight storage SOCs ( 5 ,
30
,
50
,

,

%) at 45 °C were used. To enable faster gradient calculation during the neural network training, the three steps of APTD methodology (see Section 3.2) are followed. The first step is to estimate the degradation states,
L
SEI
and
ϵ
n
a
, as a function of time for the ageing datasets considered. To achieve this, a nuanced strategy different from the suggested approaches in Section 3.2 is used, which is detailed below.

As discussed in Section 5.1, the physics-based model captures the qualitative trend of the cell capacity and anode LAM as observed in the experimental data. But, it fails to quantitatively explain the trends observed at different storage conditions using a single parameter set. However, as the qualitative behaviour matches, for any individual storage condition, it was possible to estimate a set of tunable parameters that accurately describe both the capacity drop and LAM trends quantitatively. Note that, when the observable capacity drop and LAM trends matches, it also means the model correctly explains the evolution of the degradation states for that particular ageing condition.

This means, the physics-based model can be independently tuned for each storage datasets included for neural network training to estimate the degradation state evolution for that specific ageing condition (
z
ˆ
in Section 3.2). Moreover, this approach can also provide estimates of the electrochemical states (
x
ˆ
in Section 3.2) needed for the construction of the augmented dataset, which is the second step in APTD. However, as noted in Section 3.2, we do not need to save all the electrochemical states in the augmented dataset, but rather a lower dimensional mapping of it. In this study, it is just two quantities, namely
η
SEI
and
η
̄
p
, as can be seen in the UDE degradation equations Eqs. (12) and (13). Based on this observation, we can create the augmented dataset for an ageing dataset indexed by j as

After obtaining the optimal parameters, the augmented datasets for each condition were constructed. All the time series components in the augmented dataset showed small variations over time, except for RPTs, where the potentials

This was based on the assumption that degradation primarily takes place during storage rather than during RPTs. After removing the RPT data, only 30 data-points were required to capture the time evolution of the augmented dataset components. Fig. 9 shows an example of the augmented dataset corresponding to the storage at 30% SOC and 45 °C. The reformulated Eq. (16) enable cost efficient training of the
N
1
parameters. Note that, for the ageing datasets considered for
N
1
training, the functions
η

( t )
and

̄

( t )
are precomputed and stored in the augmented dataset. Therefore, while solving Eq. (16) for the adjoint calculations, these functions can be directly accessed from the augmented dataset, instead of solving a coupled electrochemical model in parallel. This decoupling helps the solver to take time steps in the degradation time scale while solving Eq. (16), thereby addressing the parameterisation challenge detailed in Section 3.

The loss function for
N
1
training is formulated as the mean squared error (MSE) between solution of Eq. (16) and
L
SEI
values from the augmented dataset. During its optimisation, a mini-batching approach is followed, where the loss function considers only one of the eight datasets per iteration to calculate the gradient. Mini-batching reduces the computational and memory costs while also helping the optimiser to avoid local minima during training.

The quadrature adjoint technique [13] from SciMLSensitivity.jl was used for the gradient calculation of the loss function. Additionally, the Jacobian computations required for the adjoint calculations were carried out using reverse-mode AD technique implemented in Zygote.jl library [50]. Furthermore, the required ODE solving was performed using TSit5 solver from DifferentialEquations.jl library with 10−6 solver tolerance [49].

The architecture of neural network
N
1
consisted of an input layer with two neurons using a linear activation function, followed by two hidden layers with 10 neurons each and tanh activation functions, and an output layer with a single neuron using a square activation function. The ADAM optimiser from Optim.jl with a learning rate of
1 × 1
0
− 3
and 200 epochs (1600 iterations) was used for
N
1
training [51]. The optimisation was performed on a computer with Intel Xeon Gold 6148 CPU and 96 GB of RAM. With the APTD problem reformulation and mini-batching, the total optimisation of
N
1
parameters took about 30 min.

After training both the neural network using data from cells stored at 45 °C, the next step was to estimate the temperature dependent rate constants at 25 °C and 0 °C. To achieve this, we used capacity and anode LAM data from cells stored at 10% and 80% SOCs at these two temperatures. Further, the rate constants were independently parameterised at these two temperatures using a cost function same as Eq. (14). The optimised values were
κ

This Section validates the UDE degradation model against experimental data and compares its prediction accuracy with the physics-based model. Figs. 4–6 show a comparison of cell capacity predictions by both models for cells stored at 45 °C, 25 °C, and 0 °C, respectively. Similarly, Fig. S7-S9 compare the anode LAM predictions by both the models against experimental data for the same storage temperatures. Additionally, a comparison of the RMSE for model predictions on cell capacity and anode LAM is provided in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8, respectively.

First, the results from the 45 °C storage data, where the neural networks were trained, are analysed. Focusing on the capacity predictions (Fig. 4 and Fig. 7(a)), the UDE model offered stronger alignment with the experimental observations compared to the physics-based model for majority of the storage SOCs. Exceptions are 30% and 50% storage SOCs, where the physics-based model offer marginally lower RMSE in predictions (see Fig. 7). An important result is that, the UDE model effectively addresses the severe under prediction of ageing rates observed in physics-based models for storage SOCs below 30% (Fig. 4). Overall, for cells stored at 45 °C, the RMSE of the capacity predictions by the UDE model was less than 1.89%. Further, the average RMSE across all storage SOCs were 0.94%, which is a 53.4% reduction compared to the physics-based model.

Next, the results from the anode LAM predictions made for cells stored at 45 °C are analysed (Fig.S1, Fig. 8). The anode LAM predictions by the UDE model closely matched the LAM values calculated from the experimental data using DVA. Similar to the capacity predictions, the UDE model resolves the severe under prediction of anode LAM by the physics-based model for storage SOCs below 30%. Further, across all the storage SOCs except at 70% and 90% 45 °C, the UDE model offered significantly lower RMSE compared to the physics-based model.

At 70% storage SOC, the DVA LAM estimate for the fourth RPTx (see Fig.S7) is lower than the third, which is physically unlikely. This could be due to errors in peak finding during DVA, as evidenced by the large variance in LAM estimate for the fourth RPTx. Therefore, the high RMSE at 70% storage SOC can be attributed to error in DVA on fourth RPTx. The LAM predictions by the UDE model until the third RPTx matches the DVA estimates better than the physics-based model, highlighting the reliability of UDE model predictions. For cells stored at 90%, the cells showed knee effect and reached end-of-life before the fourth RPTx resulting in only three data points in Fig. S3. These cells behaved differently from the rest of the batch, and can be treated as an outlier.

Overall, for cells stored at 45 °C, ignoring 70% and 90% storage SOCs, the RMSE of the anode LAM predictions by the UDE model was less than 1.75%. Further, the average RMSE across all storage SOCs were 0.81%, which is a 57.9% reduction compared to the physics-based model.

As discussed above, the UDE model outperformed the physics-based model for calendar ageing at 45 °C, where the neural networks were trained. However, the true test for the reliability of the UDE model is to validate its ability to generalise to scenarios outside the training conditions. To this end, we analyse the UDE model predictions for calendar ageing at 25 °C and 0 °C. For these temperatures, the neural network parameters from 45 °C training were retained, and only the temperature dependent rate constants (
κ
1
and
κ
2
) were adjusted using data from 10% and 80% storage SOCs at these temperatures.

The capacity predictions by the UDE model at 25 °C (Fig. 5) and 0 °C (Fig. 6) closely match the experimental data and are considerably more accurate than the physics-based model (see Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c)). At 25 °C, the UDE model achieved an average RMSE of 0.61% and a maximum RMSE of 0.91%, which shows a 61.2% and 65% decrement respectively compared to the physics-based model. Similarly, at 0 °C, the UDE model achieved an average RMSE of 0.45% and a maximum RMSE of 0.56%, representing improvements of 61.2% and 75.6%, respectively, compared to the physics-based model. These findings successfully demonstrate the ability of the UDE degradation modelling approach to learn the underlying physics, which enable it to generalise well to out-of-training-sample scenarios.

Similar to capacity predictions, the anode LAM predictions by the UDE model closely align with the experimental data obtained from calendar ageing at 25 °C (Fig.S8) and 0 °C (Fig.S9). In comparison to the physics-based model, the LAM predictions by the UDE model generally shows lower RMSE, with a few exceptions where the physics-based model shows marginal improvements (see Fig. 8(b) and Fig. 8(c)). The maximum RMSE of the UDE model was 1.07% at 25 °C and 1.46% at 0 °C, which represent a reduction of 44.5% and 11.3% respectively compared to the physics-based model. The high accuracy in LAM predictions also demonstrate the ability of the UDE model to generalise to out-of-training-sample scenarios.

The above analysis indicate that, the UDE model demonstrates strong predictive capabilities across all the operating conditions, including scenarios outside the training data. More importantly, it outperforms the typically used physics-based model across most of the storage conditions. The cases where physics-based model offer a lower RMSE, the decrement is marginal. Additionally, the UDE model resolves the severe under prediction of capacity loss and anode LAM by the physics-based model at low storage SOCs.

In addition to degradation indicators, the UDE model also predicts evolution of the electrochemical dynamics as the cell ages. To demonstrate this, Fig. 10 presents validation of the voltage and current evolution from RPTs conducted at the beginning of life, on the 357th day, and on the 650th day for a cell stored at 50% SOC and
25
° C
. The voltage predictions of the UDE model show good agreement with the experimental results, with an average RMSE of 41.55 mV and a maximum RMSE of 46.8 mV. Furthermore, the UDE model effectively reproduces the current evolution during the constant voltage hold phase, implemented using voltage hold as an additional algebraic constraint.

In this section, we compare UDE calendar ageing modelling with existing approaches: machine learning (ML) [52], empirical [53], and physics-based models. A comparison between these approaches indicate that ML models offered the highest degree of accuracy in terms of predictions [20]. ML models use flexible function approximators to find correlations between stress factors (SOC, temperature, storage time) with capacity and resistance, directly from the data.

Despite their accuracy, ML model predictions are constrained to the training data, lack physical insights and generalisability. For instance, ML models cannot predict changes in the capacity trajectory if a different experimental protocol was used, such as different C-rate for the capacity measurement or conducting experiments under constant voltage condition instead of open circuit used for model training [54]. More importantly, ML models cannot predict how the electrochemical model behaviour evolves as the cell ages. They cannot capture changes in the SOC-OCV relationship or estimate how much energy can be extracted under specific usage conditions in an aged state.

Unlike ML models, physics-based models describe the physical mechanisms causing ageing. By performing appropriate simulations, the modeller can answer the above-mentioned questions. More importantly, predictions of physics-based models are not limited to just capacity; they can predict the evolution of all electrochemical and degradation state variables, including those that are not directly measurable. However, as shown in this article, the reliability of physics-based model predictions depends on accurately capturing the relevant physics. If the physics are incorrect, no amount of parameterisation data can improve prediction accuracy.

In the UDE model, the ability of ML models to approximate functions is used to find the relationship between state variables and their derivatives of the physics-based model. This approach ensures that the UDE model achieves high predictive accuracy of ML models while also offering physical insights that the physics-based model can offer. In addition, since the ML model is placed within the constraints of the physics-based model, the function it learns generalises well to scenarios beyond the training data.

A UDE approach also inherits some drawbacks of both methods, particularly regarding the data requirements for model parameterisation. Like physics-based models, the UDE model also require specific electrochemical parameters that often rely on expensive experiments. In addition, it will also require extensive ageing datasets, as an ML model does, to approximate the incomplete model structure for degradation dynamics.

However, note that, the requirement for a large amount of ageing data is not exclusive to UDE models. When the knowledge of the underlying physics is incomplete, traditional scientific approaches will also require extensive experimental data to learn the missing physics and incorporate it into the physics-based model structure. A UDE framework is particularly valuable in this context, as it applies machine learning to automate/assist this learning process. A UDE can also facilitate data-driven model discovery. Algorithms such as SINDy [55] enable the representation of the function (learnt by the neural networks) in a sparse parsimonious manner [13]. This form can be interpreted by experts in the domain to extract meaningful physical insights.

Another important point to note is that, as the UDE approach learns missing physics from experimental data, there is a risk that the model could learn physically implausible trends or noise present in the dataset. To mitigate this risk, the modeller must take preventive measures during data collection, model development, parameterisation, and model verification.

Data Collection: Multiple repeats of experiments should be performed to verify the validity of an observed trend. This is a standard practise in ageing studies, including the presented calendar ageing dataset, where at least 3 cells are tested under a specific ageing condition. In addition, data cleaning should be done to remove potential outliers.

Model development: Unlike purely data-driven models, most UDE components are physically interpretable, allowing the modeller to impose hard physical constraints to ensure that the neural networks in the UDE do not learn physically implausible trends. General examples of such constraints include symmetry, monotonicity, and smoothness [56].

In this study, we used several such constraints. For example, placing
L
SEI
in the denominator of Eq. (12) constrained SEI growth to be self-limiting. Furthermore, we squared the output of
N
1
in Eq. (12) to constrain the growth of SEI to be irreversible. These constraints also impose smoothness on the predictions, thereby reducing the influence of noise in capacity measurements on the model predictions.

Parameterisation: During model training, physics-based regularisation can be added to the loss function. This penalise the neural networks from learning physically infeasible solutions [56]. Furthermore, machine learning strategies such as early stopping and dropout can also be employed to prevent overfitting [57].

Model verification and validation: Validation with out-of-sample data should be carried out to confirm its generalisability. Generalisability in validation ensures that the model did not just memorise the training data (overfitting), but has learnt the underlying dynamics. In this study, we only used 8 out of 39 datasets for model training, and the model generalised very well to validation conditions, which is good indication that it did not overfit.

This article introduces the Universal Differential Equation (UDE) modelling approach for lithium-ion battery degradation modelling as an alternative to commonly used physics-based and pure-data-driven degradation modelling approaches. Our approach involves incorporating neural networks into the structure of continuum physics-based degradation models. This allows the neural network, when trained with experimental data, to approximate functions representing degradation mechanisms that are incompletely modelled in the physics-based degradation model.

Therefore, the UDE approach is particularly useful in scenarios where sufficient ageing data is available, but physics-based models, even with extensive parameterisation, fail to quantitatively fit the experimental data or replicate qualitative trends in it, due to inherent limitations in their model structure. The UDE framework addresses these shortcomings by learning optimal constitutive relations for poorly modelled degradation mechanisms within the physics-based degradation model directly from data, thereby enhancing its model structure. As a result, the UDE degradation model combines the function approximation capabilities of machine learning with the interpretability of physics-based models.

However, for the battery community to adopt the UDE approach, this article emphasises that the high cost associated with the parameterisation of the UDE model needs to be addressed. To this end, we combined the newly developed APTD method and mini-batching, which enables faster and memory-efficient parameterisation.

Furthermore, by using calendar ageing as an example, this article demonstrates how to formulate UDE degradation models and then parameterise the resultant model using the newly proposed method. After parameterisation, the UDE calendar ageing model was validated using an extensive in-house calendar ageing dataset that spans 39 storage conditions (13 storage SOCs at
0
°
C,
25
°
C and
45
°
C), with an average storage period of 686 days and three cells per condition.

Model validation showcases the ability of the UDE model to capture the degradation behaviour across all operating conditions. The average RMSE across all storage SOCs for capacity prediction, by the UDE model, were 0.94%, 0.61%, and 0.45% at 45 °C, 25 °C, and 0 °C, respectively. In comparison, the widely used physics-based model had higher RMSE of 2.02%, 1.57% and 1.16% respectively. The UDE model significantly outperformed the physics-based model in terms of accuracy of capacity predictions as well as anode LAM predictions. Note that, the neural networks in the UDE model were only trained using the 45 °C dataset. The high accuracy across the other two temperatures demonstrates the ability of the neural networks to learn the underlying physics, which enable generalisation to out-of-training-sample scenarios.
